name: Midland Railway EMU (1 Car)
bio: Very early experiments in AC Multiple Units were built for the Lancaster-Morecambe-Heysham line, and were remarkably successful. Various lengths of unit were available, with twice as many single-ended driving trailers built as double-ended driving motors were.

bio: The LB&SCR (Later SR) “CP” EMUs were a nightmare of different build dates and vehicle sizes, designed for use on the “Elevated Electric” line into London.

name: LB&SCR “CW” EMU
bio: The LB&SCR (Later SR) “Coulsden & Wallington” EMUs were a rather more uniform set of vehicles than their previous units, and included a large dedicated luggage vehicle in the centre of the formation. They were soon converted to standard 3rd rail power supply.

They were soon converted to standard 3rd rail power supply.

name: LB&SCR “Milk Van” MLV
bio: The motor luggage vans powering the CW EMUs were usable locomotives in their own right, and could operate independantly if needed.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The motor luggage vans powering the CW EMUs were usable locomotives in their own right, and could operate independantly if needed.

name: Ruston and Hornsby 48DS
bio: A “Really Useless Engine” – these small, simple industrial locomotives saw service in many industrial yards but were almost never seen on the boader network.

name: Ruston and Hornsby 88DS
bio: A mild upgrade in power made the Ruston 88 significantly more effective than it’s little brother, without vastly increasing cost.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}A mild upgrade in power made the Ruston 88 significantly more effective than it’s little brother, without vastly increasing cost.

name: BR Class 201 “Thumper” “4S”
bio: Class 201 ‘Hastings’ Units were constructed to a narrow loading gauge to work through a number of limited clearance tunnels in the southern region. Southern DEMUs of the time had their engines mounted above the frames.

bio: Class 205 units were a relatively numerous class of high-density non-corridor multiple units that worked services across Hampshire. In many ways they were the classic ‘Thumper’ unit.

name: BREL Class 210 (4 car)
bio: The Class 210 was an early design for the modernization of DMU services that became the Sprinter series. A diesel-electric design to a very high specification, but through gangways and above-solebar engines like the classic ‘Thumper’. This unit represents 210 001, the 4-car test unit.

name: MC Class 251 "Blue Pullman"
bio: The Blue Pullman is an express diesel multiple unit, designed to tempt motorists from their cars with a luxurious inter-city service. This is the 6-car “Midland Region” layout.

name: BR Class 302 “AM2”
bio: A suburban Mk1 EMU that mainly operated out of East London on the LT&S line. Here we represent the refurbished condition of these units, which lasted until 1998.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}A suburban Mk1 EMU that mainly operated out of East London on the LT&S line. Here we represent the refurbished condition of these units, which lasted until 1998.

name: BR Class 302/9
bio: A later conversion of the Class 302 to a parcels unit involved the addition of roller doors to the bodyside for faster loading and unloading. The prototypes were also painted in a striking Royal Mail red livery.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.3 tiles{}{SILVER}A later conversion of the Class 302 to a parcels unit involved the addition of roller doors to the bodyside for faster loading and unloading. The prototypes were also painted in a striking Royal Mail red livery.

name: BR Class 309/1 “Clacton Express”
bio: The ‘Clacton’ units were iconic express EMUs of the GEML’s early electrification. This subclass represents a 2 car formation, used to boost 2nd class capacity and power on peak time services.

bio: The ‘Clacton’ units were iconic express EMUs of the GEML’s early electrification. This subclass represents a 4 car formation complete with the famous ‘Griddle Car’ restaurant.

name: Tyne and Wear Metrocar
bio: Tyne and Wear’s ‘Metrocars’ were built by Metro-Cammell to serve the Newcastle metro system in the northeast of England – an amalgamation of a number of suburban lines and newly built tunnels.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.0 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Tyne and Wear’s ‘Metrocars’ were built by Metro-Cammell to serve the Newcastle metro system in the northeast of England – an amalgamation of a number of suburban lines and newly built tunnels.

name: BR Class 411 “4CEP”
bio: The archetypal Mk1 Express EMU – 4CEP units were an evolution of the earlier southern 4COR units. 133 were built and they enjoyed a long career into the early 2000s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}750V DC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}3.0 tiles{}{SILVER}The archetypal Mk1 Express EMU – 4CEP units were an evolution of the earlier southern 4COR units. 133 were built and they enjoyed a long career into the early 2000s.

name: BR Class 419 “MLV”
bio: The Class 419 Motor Luggage Van was used to extend the luggage capacity of express services and was capable of running on it’s own batteries within non-electrified areas.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}750V DC / Battery {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 419 Motor Luggage Van was used to extend the luggage capacity of express services and was capable of running on it’s own batteries within non-electrified areas.

name: BR Class 489 “GLV”
bio: A late conversion of the 2HAP driving motor to a cab and luggage car for Gatwick Express services
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}750V DC {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}A late conversion of the 2HAP driving motor to a cab and luggage car for Gatwick Express services

name: Leyland LEV1
bio: One of a number of prototype vehicles built for the project that eventually delivered the Pacer family, Leyland’s attempt to reuse as many bus components as possible is on full display in the LEV1.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.6 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}One of a number of prototype vehicles built for the project that eventually delivered the Pacer family, Leyland’s attempt to reuse as many bus components as possible is on full display in the LEV1.

name: Leyland Class 141 "Pacer"
bio: The Class 141 is a multiple unit built from Leyland National bus parts. While cheap and light, it is criticised for its poor ride quality.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.2 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% penalty for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Class 141 is a multiple unit built from Leyland National bus parts. While cheap and light, it is criticised for its poor ride quality.

name: BREL Class 150 "Sprinter"
bio: The Sprinter replaced first-generation DMUs. Like other second-generation units it was based on the Mark 3 bodyshell. The name refers to its faster acceleration compared to the units it replaced.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}diesel_RAILThe Sprinter replaced first-generation DMUs. Like other second-generation units it was based on the Mark 3 bodyshell. name: Metro-Cammell Class 151
bio: A class of 2 prototype DMUs built to compete for the contract that became the Class 150 fleet purchase. These units suffered with gearbox issues and the BREL type won the contest instead.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}3{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.3 tiles{}{SILVER}A class of 2 prototype DMUs built to compete for the contract that became the Class 150 fleet purchase. These units suffered with gearbox issues and the BREL type won the contest instead.

name: Leyland/Hunslet Class 153 "Super Sprinter"
bio: The Class 153 was a conversion of earlier Class 155 units, 35 of which were split in two with small cabs added at the inner ends.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 153 was a conversion of earlier Class 155 units, 35 of which were split in two with small cabs added at the inner ends.

name: Leyland/Hunslet Class 153 "Mail Sprinter"
bio: What if the class 153s were converted for parcels service rather than passenger use? This preceds the later use of the 153 as supplementary bike carriages or ‘active travel’ vehicles.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}What if the class 153s were converted for parcels service rather than passenger use? This preceds the later use of the 153 as supplementary bike carriages or ‘active travel’ vehicles.

name: Metro-Cammell Class 156 "Super Sprinter"
bio: The Class 156 is a member of the Sprinter family designed for longer journeys, with single doors at each end rather than the metro-style doors of the Class 150s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 156 is a member of the Sprinter family designed for longer journeys, with single doors at each end rather than the metro-style doors of the Class 150s.

name: BREL Class 158 "Express Sprinter"
bio: The Class 158 has many improvements over earlier generation Sprinters, including air conditioning and a more comfortable ride.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Class 158 has many improvements over earlier generation Sprinters, including air conditioning and a more comfortable ride.

bio: When both Class 158 and Class 156 vehicles are unavailable, it’s possible to run hybrid units of the type, and these have been documented in passenger service on many occasions.

name: Bombardier Class 170 "Turbostar"
bio: The Turbostar is a further development of the BR "Networker" units. It is used for regional and long distance services.

name: BREL APT-S Driving Motor
bio: The APT-S was a rework of the APT concept towards more conventional rolling stock, with a driving motor car, articulated trailers and a DVT.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The APT-S was a rework of the APT concept towards more conventional rolling stock, with a driving motor car, articulated trailers and a DVT.

name: BREL APT-S/Q Driving Van Trailer
bio: The APT-Q was proposed to rationalise existing APT-P stock into usable 6-car tains, with a single non-driving power car and a new-build DVT.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The APT-Q was proposed to rationalise existing APT-P stock into usable 6-car tains, with a single non-driving power car and a new-build DVT.

name: BREL Class 43 “HST” (Buffers)
bio: To facilitate the testing and introduction of the Class 91 fleet prior to the availability of Mark 4 Driving Van Trailers, 8 HST Power Cars were converted to act as control vehicles for electric locomotives.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}To facilitate the testing and introduction of the Class 91 fleet prior to the availability of Mark 4 Driving Van Trailers, 8 HST Power Cars were converted to act as control vehicles for electric locomotives.

name: BR Mark 3 Driving Trailer
bio: A conversion of the Mk3 coach to incorporate a driving cab was proposed before the Polmont derailment put a stop to the idea of having passengers in the leading vehicle of locomotive-propelled trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}A conversion of the Mk3 coach to incorporate a driving cab was proposed before the Polmont derailment put a stop to the idea of having passengers in the leading vehicle of locomotive-propelled trains.

name: BREL Class 43 DVT (Buffers)
bio: A depowered Class 43, proposed as a conversion from surplus power cars and now suitable for carrying baggage.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}A depowered Class 43, proposed as a conversion from surplus power cars and now suitable for carrying baggage.

name: BREL E-HST
bio: A proposed electric version of the Class 43 ‘HST’ Power Car was oft-discussed after the failure of the APT program.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}A proposed electric version of the Class 43 ‘HST’ Power Car was oft-discussed after the failure of the APT program.

name: BREL Class 88
bio: The Class 88 was a proposed re-use of the Class 58 design, dropping the diesel engine for a 25kv Overhead power supply.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 88 was a proposed re-use of the Class 58 design, dropping the diesel engine for a 25kv Overhead power supply.

name: BREL Class 38 "Mini Bone"
bio: The Class 38 was a proposed cut-down Class 58 using a 2000hp-class diesel engine. If built, these would likely have displaced the oldest remaining Type 2 and Type 3 locomotives on the network, replacing Class 31s and Class 37s on freight duties.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 38 was a proposed cut-down Class 58 using a 2000hp-class diesel engine. If built, these would likely have displaced the oldest remaining Type 2 and Type 3 locomotives on the network, replacing Class 31s and Class 37s on freight duties.

name: BREL Class 442 "Wessex Electric"
bio: The Class 442 holds the speed record for a third rail EMU at 108mph. Unlike most of its generation of Mk3-based multiple units, it uses the full-length carriage bodyshell.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}750V DC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.1 tiles{}{YELLOW}10% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Class 442 holds the speed record for a third rail EMU at 108mph. Unlike most of its generation of Mk3-based multiple units, it uses the full-length carriage bodyshell.

name: BREL Class 442/8 "Wessex Electric"
bio: What if the Class 442 had a dual-voltage conversion to allow through-running expresses and an end to 3rd rail power supply?

name: GEC-Alsthom Class 373 "Eurostar"
bio: An adaption of the french TGV to UK Loading gauge, and designed to run through the Channel Tunnel onto 750V DC track for a slow crawl into south London, the Class 373 continues to run international services.

bio: The first of a number of cancelled cross-channel service concepts, the Regional Eurostar sets never operated as intended, but were used for relief services on the ECML and as conventional TGV sets in France.

bio: Amongst the last passenger vehicles built at the former BREL Holgate Road works, the Class 365s began to be delivered at the very end of British Rail operations.

name: BREL Class 465 "Networker"
bio: The BREL Networker was built by three different companies (ABB, Metro-Cammell and BREL) with minor detail differences between each manufacturer's output.

name: BREL Class 466 “Networker”
bio: The Class 466 was a shorter Class 465, used for small branch line service and to strengthen peak services up to 10 cars as needed.

name: EE Class 37/6 "Tractor"
bio: Originally to power the “Nightstar” european sleeper services away from electrified lines, this sub-class of 12 locomotives mostly saw use as rescue and freight locomotives with other operators.

name: BR Class 73/9 “Ultra73”
bio: These re-engined and modernized Class 73s retained their ability to run from the third rail but received diesel engines capable of operating at an equivalent power level.

name: Alstom Class 458/5 "5JUP"
bio: The suburban conversion of ex-class 460 trains, now through-gangwayed and in 5 car formations. 4 of the former Class 460 baggage vehicles were scrapped as part of the conversion program.

name: Siemens Class 374 ‘Velaro D’
bio: Eurostar’s 2010s replacement for the Class 373 units, which were by this point corroding and becoming rather unreliable took a number of years to enter service but allowed Eurostar to begin service to new destinations in Europe.

name: Hitachi Class 800/0 "Azuma"
bio: The Class 800 "Azuma" (on LNER) or "IET" (on GWR) is a high speed bi-mode unit based on the Hitachi AT300 series. It is designed to replace HST and Class 91 hauled services on Intercity routes.

name: Hitachi Class 801/1 "Azuma"
bio: The Class 801 "Azuma" is LNER’s primary Electric Multiple Unit, procured to replace the Class 91s. It has a single diesel engine for use within depots and during emergencies, but is normally restricted to electrified areas.

name: Hitachi Class 802/0 "IET"
bio: The Class 802 "IET" is a high speed bi-mode unit based on the Hitachi AT300 series. Compared to the Class 800, they have uprated engines and larger fuel tanks, to allow them to operate more easily in non-electrified areas such as Cornwall.

bio: The Class 802 "IET" is a high speed bi-mode unit based on the Hitachi AT300 series. Compared to the Class 800, they have uprated engines and larger fuel tanks, to allow them to operate more easily in non-electrified areas such as Cornwall.

name: Siemens Class 717 "Desiro City"
bio: The Class 717 ‘Desiro City’ units are variants of the Class 700 Thameslink units, with front emergency exit doors to allow them to work the Northern City Line. They replaced earlier PEP units on King’s Cross suburban services.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC / 750V DC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}6{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The Class 717 ‘Desiro City’ units are variants of the Class 700 Thameslink units, with front emergency exit doors to allow them to work the Northern City Line. They replaced earlier PEP units on King’s Cross suburban services.

name: Stadler Class 745 "FLIRT"
bio: Stadler’s Class 745 units replaced Class 90 hauled trains on the GEML. The semi-articulated design was required to meet crashworthiness specifications.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}12{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}9.0 tiles{}{SILVER}Stadler’s Class 745 units replaced Class 90 hauled trains on the GEML. The semi-articulated design was required to meet crashworthiness specifications.

name: Hitachi Class 810 "Aurora"
bio: The Class 810 "Aurora" is a variant on the class 800 series, built to a shorter vehicle length to suit station lengths on the Midland Mainline. It finally began to displace Class 222 DEMUs at the end of 2025.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}25kV AC / Diesel {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}5{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}4.1 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Class 810 "Aurora" is a variant on the class 800 series, built to a shorter vehicle length to suit station lengths on the Midland Mainline. It finally began to displace Class 222 DEMUs at the end of 2025.

name: RCH Salt Wagon
bio: The standard RCH Salt Wagon design of 1907 was long-lived in private ownership and saw some use beyond sodium chloride, which was largely used as part of the chemical industry.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The standard RCH Salt Wagon design of 1907 was long-lived in private ownership and saw some use beyond sodium chloride, which was largely used as part of the chemical industry.

name: “Presflo” Powder Hopper
bio: The Presflo covered hopper was designed specifically to carry cement powder, being unloaded via a pressurized air connection and liquefaction of the cargo.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The Presflo covered hopper was designed specifically to carry cement powder, being unloaded via a pressurized air connection and liquefaction of the cargo.

name: PCA “Vee” Powder Tanker
bio: The PCA tanker, with it’s notable depressed-center ‘vee’ appearance, could carry large amounts of cement and was a common sight in mixed freight trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The PCA tanker, with it’s notable depressed-center ‘vee’ appearance, could carry large amounts of cement and was a common sight in mixed freight trains.

name: JPA Powder Tanker
bio: By the 2000s the BR-era 2-axle cement tankers then in use were beginning to show their age. They were replaced by these large bogie tank wagons built in Germany, with a distinctive fish-belly shape.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}By the 2000s the BR-era 2-axle cement tankers then in use were beginning to show their age. They were replaced by these large bogie tank wagons built in Germany, with a distinctive fish-belly shape.

name: Midland Railway Brake Van
bio: This Midland Railway design of Brake Van was also built by the LMS - over 800 were constructed and they were still extant into the early 1960s.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}This Midland Railway design of Brake Van was also built by the LMS - over 800 were constructed and they were still extant into the early 1960s.

name: LMS Brake Van
bio: The native design of LMS Brake Van had a sizable veranda at both ends. Once again, they lasted well into BR ownership as part of Engineers’ trains.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The native design of LMS Brake Van had a sizable veranda at both ends. Once again, they lasted well into BR ownership as part of Engineers’ trains.

name: LMS Grain Hopper
bio: The LMS Grain Hooper was later standardised into the BR COVGRAIN standard design. Long-distance bulk grain service in covered hoppers was a significant efficiency gain over shipping bagged grain by the vanload.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The LMS Grain Hooper was later standardised into the BR COVGRAIN standard design. Long-distance bulk grain service in covered hoppers was a significant efficiency gain over shipping bagged grain by the vanload.

name: SR “Queen Mary” Brake Van
bio: The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses.

name: SR “Queen Mary” Brake Van (Alternate)
bio: The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses. This variant is in the alternate company colour scheme.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}The first SR ‘Queen Mary’ Brake vans converted from LBSCR ‘Milk Van’ MLVs, and lasted in departmental and limited freight service into the 2000s! A shed on rails with a stove and a desk has 1000 uses. This variant is in the alternate company colour scheme.

name: BR Grain Hopper
bio: This is a representative BR GRAINFIT wagon, a vacuum-braked bulk hopper derived from the 1930s LMS design.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}This is a representative BR GRAINFIT wagon, a vacuum-braked bulk hopper derived from the 1930s LMS design.

name: “Grainflow” Covered Hopper
bio: The BR ‘Whiskey Covhops’ were a common sight on long-distance freight trains from southern England, headed for the distilleries of Scotland.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.4 tiles{}{SILVER}The BR ‘Whiskey Covhops’ were a common sight on long-distance freight trains from southern England, headed for the distilleries of Scotland.

name: “Polybulk” Covered Hopper
bio: Large bogie covered hoppers first appeared in Britain in the mid 1970s. These were frequently privately owned and registered as international vehicles. This slab-sided ‘Polybulk’ example was built in France.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}Large bogie covered hoppers first appeared in Britain in the mid 1970s. These were frequently privately owned and registered as international vehicles. This slab-sided ‘Polybulk’ example was built in France.

name: BR VIX Ferry Van
bio: BR’s two-axle Ferry Vans were built for interoperation with european service via cross-channel ferry services, allowing British companies to send goods directly to continental customers.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}BR’s two-axle Ferry Vans were built for interoperation with european service via cross-channel ferry services, allowing British companies to send goods directly to continental customers.

name: Cargowaggon IWB Van
bio: The prominent CARGOWAGGON-lettered vans began arriving in international traffic during the late 1970s and 1980s. These large goods wagons were reliable and high-capacity. The whole hood lifts and slides to permit forklift access to the wagon.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The prominent CARGOWAGGON-lettered vans began arriving in international traffic during the late 1970s and 1980s. These large goods wagons were reliable and high-capacity. The whole hood lifts and slides to permit forklift access to the wagon.

name: Cargowaggon IZA Twin Van
bio: Built as permanently-coupled pairs of two-axle wagons, supposedly to capture an EU subsidy, these smaller cargowaggon sets continue to see service within the UK.
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}2{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}1.0 tiles{}{SILVER}Built as permanently-coupled pairs of two-axle wagons, supposedly to capture an EU subsidy, these smaller cargowaggon sets continue to see service within the UK.

name: Cargowaggon IGA Flat Wagon
bio: The basic chassis of the Cargowaggon van adapted naturally to a sturdy, high-capacity flat wagon, used for steel, wood and other cargo, then later adapted for departmental work carrying welded rail and as match wagons.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The basic chassis of the Cargowaggon van adapted naturally to a sturdy, high-capacity flat wagon, used for steel, wood and other cargo, then later adapted for departmental work carrying welded rail and as match wagons.

name: LNWR Sleeping Car
bio: Early 20th century sleeping cars were often heavier and larger than their daytime counterparts. This represents a wooden-bodied, 6-axle sleeping coach of the golden era of train travel, in reality there were many variants built in many batches.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Early 20th century sleeping cars were often heavier and larger than their daytime counterparts. This represents a wooden-bodied, 6-axle sleeping coach of the golden era of train travel, in reality there were many variants built in many batches.

name: CIWL Type F Sleeping Car
bio: The Franco-Belgian Compagnie Internationale des Wagons-Lits ran a private sleeper service from London, via the Southern Railway and a cross-channel ferry, to connect to the Orient Express in Paris. A fleet of 25 all-steel sleeping cars was built for the service.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Franco-Belgian Compagnie Internationale des Wagons-Lits ran a private sleeper service from London, via the Southern Railway and a cross-channel ferry, to connect to the Orient Express in Paris. A fleet of 25 all-steel sleeping cars was built for the service.

name: CIWL Fourgon Baggage Car
bio: These two-axle baggage cars were a fixture of the night ferry service, running with the sleeping cars on the 11 hour schedule from London to Paris.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{SILVER}These two-axle baggage cars were a fixture of the night ferry service, running with the sleeping cars on the 11 hour schedule from London to Paris.

name: BR Mk1 BG (Prototype)
bio: The standard parcels and baggage car on British Rail for decades, the Mk1 Brake Gangwayed was extremely versatile. This represents a standard 57’ frame example, making it noticably shorter than the standard Mk1 coach.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}The standard parcels and baggage car on British Rail for decades, the Mk1 Brake Gangwayed was extremely versatile. This represents a standard 57’ frame example, making it noticably shorter than the standard Mk1 coach.

name: BR Mk1 BG (B4 Bogies)
bio: In the 1980s a number of BG vehicles were upgraded with B4 bogies and cleared for travel at 110mph, to provide additional capacity within fast passenger services, which were typically run with Mark 2 stock.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}In the 1980s a number of BG vehicles were upgraded with B4 bogies and cleared for travel at 110mph, to provide additional capacity within fast passenger services, which were typically run with Mark 2 stock.

name: BR Mk1 Super BG
bio: By the 90s parcels traffic was in decline on the railway, and increasingly movement focused on fixed flows between mail terminals. As a result, a number of vehicles were rebuilt with roller shutter doors for faster loading.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.7 tiles{}{SILVER}By the 90s parcels traffic was in decline on the railway, and increasingly movement focused on fixed flows between mail terminals. As a result, a number of vehicles were rebuilt with roller shutter doors for faster loading.

name: BR FVX Carflat
bio: The ‘Carflat’ Wagons were simply coach underframes with wooden decks and low sides added, normally used to convey assembled vehicles. In many cases, these were used in passenger train formations, in the Motorail service as part of sleeper trains.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.8 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}{SILVER}The ‘Carflat’ Wagons were simply coach underframes with wooden decks and low sides added, normally used to convey assembled vehicles. In many cases, these were used in passenger train formations, in the Motorail service as part of sleeper trains.

name: BR XMA Cartic-4
bio: Large and articulated, the cartic 4 sets were built for motorail services and used to move new cars from factories to export.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}4{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}2.4 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel (for passengers){}{SILVER}Large and articulated, the cartic 4 sets were built for motorail services and used to move new cars from factories to export.

name: BR Mk1 Sleeper
bio: The Mk1 Sleeper was built in relatively large numbers. The type was not especially popular with passengers, but the service was still an improvement on sleeping in ordinary compartment stock of the time.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk1 Sleeper was built in relatively large numbers. The type was not especially popular with passengers, but the service was still an improvement on sleeping in ordinary compartment stock of the time.

name: BR Mk3 Sleeper
bio: The Mk3 Sleeper was a significant upgrade over the previous stock – being air conditioned and air sprung. Two berths per cabin unfortunately give these vehicles a low capacity. This represents a typical Sleeper, Either Class, Pantry (SLEP) vehicle.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk3 Sleeper was a significant upgrade over the previous stock – being air conditioned and air sprung. Two berths per cabin unfortunately give these vehicles a low capacity. This represents a typical Sleeper, Either Class, Pantry (SLEP) vehicle.

name: EPS “Nightstar” Sleeper (7 Car)
bio: The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. The 7-car sets were intended for service to the West Country, Wales and Scotland.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}7{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}5.7 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}{SILVER}The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. The 7-car sets were intended for service to the West Country, Wales and Scotland.

name: EPS “Nightstar” Sleeper (8 Car)
bio: The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. 8-car sets were intended for flagship service between London and major European cities.
brake_text: {YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}
additional_text: {BLACK}Cars: {GOLD}8{}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}6.5 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% long distance bonus, if combined with generator car or 5000hp+ locomotive{}{SILVER}The European ‘Nightstar’ service never got off the ground and the Mark 4 derived coaches were eventually exported to Canada. 8-car sets were intended for flagship service between London and major European cities.

name: EPS “Nightstar” Generator Car
bio: The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.
additional_text: {BLACK}Powered by: {GOLD}Diesel {}{BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{SILVER}The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.

bio: The Nightstar rolling stock was so power-hungry that a number of Mk3 Sleepers were converted with 450 kVA generator sets for use when away from electrified areas.

name: CAF Mk5 Sleeper (Berths)
bio: The Mk5 sleeper trains were procured to replace Mk3 sleepers. With the end of double berths, these trains have an even lower capacity than what they replaced!
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}50% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}The Mk5 sleeper trains were procured to replace Mk3 sleepers. With the end of double berths, these trains have an even lower capacity than what they replaced!

name: CAF Mk5 Sleeper (Seated)
bio: Mk5 sleeper services make up for their pathetic berth count with conventional seated stock, featuring a 2+1 airline style seating and not enough recline in the seats.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}20% bonus for long distance travel{}{SILVER}Mk5 sleeper services make up for their pathetic berth count with conventional seated stock, featuring a 2+1 airline style seating and not enough recline in the seats.

name: CAF Mk5 Sleeper (Club Car)
bio: The Mk5 ‘Club Car’ is the dining facility for sleeper services, but the 30 seats within are not bookable for travel.
additional_text: {BLACK}Length: {GOLD}0.9 tiles{}{YELLOW}25% long distance bonus for consist{}{SILVER}The Mk5 ‘Club Car’ is the dining facility for sleeper services, but the 30 seats within are not bookable for travel.
